sentences.text: Well, I wasn't allowed to go to the camp, as I told you before.
We had to stay only in the villa.
However, I mentioned before, I used to sneak out to, to visit my mother.
I took chances.
I did have certain privileges because I worked for the commander from camp-- all the Jewish police that was in camp.
We had a Jewish police there.
All the SS men knew me.
And many times, I could get a favor here and there, meaning going to the entrance of the-- to the, to the barracks to visit my mother.
Nobody questioned me.
I could-- I-- many time, I carried some food under my arm for my, for my mother.
And same thing with the SS.
If they would see me going, they would not report me.
The, the minute Goeth would leave for the office-- he was most of the time in the office.
Most of the time he was in office during the day, unless he would go into the camp because he felt like killing or punishing or they were hanging people.
He would give the order.
He would be there, because he rather enjoyed that.
One day, I heard a knock on my door-- on my window from my room.
While the people were marching to work, one of my friends came to tell me that they took my mother at night for deportation.
I ran very quickly.
As I mentioned to you before, there was no problem for me to get her out.
Because if I would just approach somebody who was watching those people, they saw me.
They would take my mother out.
That happened a few times.
Unfortunately, somebody denounced that boy, my friend, to Goeth.
Because they ordered to take him and put him in a cell.
For 24 hours, he stood.
He couldn't turn or sit, just standing in his cell.
And after 24 hours, he received 25 lashes on his back side.
You know, we met after the war.
And we talked about it.
And I felt just so bad.
And we, we hugged each other and, and cried actually, thinking how dreadful, how close to death he was, that he wasn't killed.
The punishment was severe.
And we never found out how and who denounced him.
But at the same time, I saved my mother from the deportation.
One time, I, I heard his-- him name calling my name.
I'm running up.
You know, I never walked up one or two steps-- two, three steps in one time, running up.
But no matter how fast I would come to his calling, it was never fast enough.
There would be always that slam on my cheek.
And his hands were so big that it wasn't my cheek alone.
It was my, my ear, part of my head.
And it was always swinging to one side or the other.
It was [? never ?] good for him.
I saw him standing near the big window with a machine gun.
And he said to me, look at those pigs.
There were people down, down the hill.
They were digging ditches, carrying rocks.
It was-- they were standing for some reason.
And he says, if they don't start working within few seconds, they're going be all dead.
He's holding that machine gun.
Well, if talking about it now, I feel like I'm running.
I ran out so fast out of that villa.
And you know, I didn't even go down-- there was a, there was a road going down the hill where people were supposedly going.
I turn on my back.
And I slid down that, that steep hill to go as fast down to warn them that he's watching.
Because he would have started shooting.
I'm sure.
If I wouldn't have slid down that hill, there would be shooting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I could go on and on telling you stories like that.
But [SIGHS] they so gruesome.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Especially when I keep thinking about those dogs, how many time I saw the flesh of the body on the road and him standing with a big smile and bending his head to the side-- this was the way he would feel very content [BACKROUND NOISE] with himself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, [BACKROUND NOISE] it wasn't very easy to keep telling you that everything that happened, I really believe that God was with me.
Because at that point, where he came looking for me to kill me with his gun in his hand, and I wasn't physically there.
Because prior to it, he came down.
And he said, Susanna, Hana, you have to get out of the villa.
Because there are rumors are going that Jews live in my house.
And that's wrong.
So you have to get out.
So we moved to the next building, a small house that was occupied by SS women, most horrible women.
They were dreadful.
They were beating people.
They were treacherous.
Do you remember their names?
No.
I had nothing to do with those people, because I lived only in a villa.
And the only place I knew that they are upstairs, because we occupied the room in the cellar again in that house.
And I saw the women upstairs.
But I had no nothing to do with them.
I was actually cut out of everything.
And I have no recollection what happened in camp.
Because I was only in a villa.
The only time I went up to the camp is when I visited my mother.
My grandmother was alive, some of my cousins.
Very quickly I would hug my mom.
I would just stay there for a few minutes.
Sometimes I would bring some food.
And I ran very quickly down, because-- you know, it wasn't the fear of, of being killed.
It was always my fear that he will take my sisters, my family, and make me-- and because this is what the-- this is what the Germans did.
This is what they actually did.
They made you suffer to watch how they murdered your dearest, closest people to you.
And that was my greatest fear, that if he will see me going there, he will take my mom.
He will take my sisters.
He will take my grandma.
And he will torture them so I can see it.
Because that's what he liked to do, torture, you know?
What happened to your mother?
My mother became ill.
She had a complication.
She had pneumonia.
And you really couldn't stay in the barrack and rest, because they took you right away to death.
So I tried to-- and naturally, there was hardly any medication.
I got some medication from another German civilian that brought me a couple of times.
But she really had to be on more medications.
She had complication.
And she didn't rest.
She was going with fever to work.
I got her a job very close to her barracks so she wouldn't have to walk far, in a, in a washroom.
I-- everybody that I approached, if I ask for favor, they were, they were always there for me.
Everybody knew me.
Everybody in Plaszow knew that I work for, for Amon Goeth.
And they knew what a horrible, horrible person he was, if he was-- if I can call him a person.
I mean, people feared him.
Anything you read about experiences with Goeth, it, it, it, it's just fearful.
It's just-- it's, it's-- you can't describe this man.
You can't describe him.
He was-- [SIGHS] it's just so hard to find words to describe him.
It's-- this is what makes me so frustrated to talk about it, because I can't describe that it's just-- it doesn't exist.
I mean, it just doesn't.
He-- people-- [DRINKS] [SIGHS] Everybody knew that being under his roof, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it must be so horrible to live day and night.
I mean, even in my sleep, when I slept still in the villa, when I heard his footsteps upstairs, I already shaking inside me.
So you know, I lived in fear, constant fear.
You asked me before if I had food.
There was plenty food.
I couldn't eat the food.
There was a constant-- such fear.
I lived in constant fear.
I mean, you never knew what the next moment would be with him.
There was so much beating going on, so much humiliating me constantly.

You know, I think that thinking back, [SIGHS] if I wouldn't have such a strong background of so much love and, and self-dignity, I think I probably-- he would have succeed to, to [?mesh?] it all up in me.
Because the names he called me, I almost started to think, am I really that bad?
But I knew I wasn't.
I knew I was a good girl.
Did he ever touch you?
Hit me-- he hit me.
He kicked me.
He pushed me down.
Other than the beatings, in a, in a, in a--
Oh, no, he had such hate for us.
There was such embedded hate.
The look even told you that.
I mean, the way he looked at us with such a disgust, it was like we were dirt.
There was no such thing ever.
I don't even know who would even think that way.
He had so much dislike or so much animosity toward us.
It's just, I don't even know how anybody could even imagine that anything else could even come to his mind.

We were so scared that, you know, she always used to tell me, always used to tell me from the beginning-- as a matter of fact, I'm going to tell you a incident where she taught me, just don't explain.
Just say, jawohl, jawohl.
It means yes, yes.
Like order, you know-- don't explain.
Don't explain.
Every time we tried to explain, there was so much beating that you have no idea.
I mean, it was like double of the amount, you know?
It was like, like irritable to him that, that you have the right to speak up.
This one time, he came down.
It was like about 3 o'clock in the morning.
He had no company.
But he was up with her-- excuse me.
And he came down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She told me he was wearing-- he had no shirt on.
But he had a gun in his hands.
And he say, he ask, where is Susanna?
And Helena said, she went to sleep.
I was sleeping in the next house.

All of a sudden, about 30, 30, 40 minutes later, because when I looked at the clock, I saw it was quarter to 4:00, I feel like somebody is trying to, to, to hit the door of, of that room that we slept in.
And I came to the door.
I opened the door.
And she fell on top of me.
Her one eye was closed up.
She was completely swollen.
And, and, and she was so sore.
And, and she said, just put me down, put me down on a bed.
Put me down.
I'm bringing her on her bed.
And she said to me, thank God you weren't there.
He came for you.
He wanna kill you.
Instead, he beat her up with this gun.
He said-- and she couldn't talk.
I said, what happened?
She said she told him.
She told him.
I'm going to tell you what happened.
She said to me, Helen, when you go in the morning, don't try to explain.
Just say jawohl, jawohl.
Well, I slowly started to get dressed.
And I went to the villa.
What happened is about a month prior to it, I was on the top of the-- on the third floor, dusting near the window.
It was a round window with a flower pot.
All of the sudden, as I'm looking down, I saw women with white kerchief on their heads marching in fives toward the end of the camp.
And I realized there must be a deportation.
And I only saw women.
By that time, my mother died already.
And I want to go back to you and tell you that when my mother died, my boyfriend Adam was so close to my mother.
He loved her so much, because his parents were already dead.
And she took such liking to him.
And she somehow always kept talking about that-- she was rather religious.
And she didn't want to be burned.
And they did burn the bodies in Plaszow.
She says, I don't want to be burned.
I don't want to be cremated.
I want to be buried.
And my boyfriend, because his work was at night, he worked at night, he took her body.
He carried her-- she was already very thin at the time when she was dying.
He carried her on, on his back, on his shoulder.
And he buried her.
And he told me that he put a bottle with her name.
And he told me exactly not far from the kitchen, he exactly told me when she was buried.
After the war, me and my sister, my older sister, when we went to Krakow, we went and we tried-- we took somebody to dig and dig to find-- we didn't find her body.
Because before the German were leaving, they, they burned bodies day and night, not to leave any traces.
So probably they must have dug out her body as well.
We couldn't find it.
Going back to what I told you about that night, I was dusting the-- at that window when I saw the women marching.
I just thought that it could be that my sisters are going-- being in that transport.
I came running down.
And I ran to the drawer.
I grabbed a white kerchief.
And I said to Helen, I have to go.
Because in case Adele and Betty are in the, in the transport, I'm going.
She says, Helen, don't go.
Don't go.
Let's see what we can do with her.
She was sleeping upstairs, his mistress.
I said, no, no, I can't take chances.
I'm going.
She was trying so hard to hold me back.
I said, no, I have to go.
In case they are going, I'm going with them.
I had no one anymore.
And I ran down.
And you know, I probably lost my head.
Because I was running so fast.
And when I saw them in the middle of the transport, I ran straight to Goeth.
He was in front of the cattle cars, right in front of the entrance of the camp.
And I probably lost my head, because I thought that if I tell him that my sisters are there, maybe I am working for him such a long time, maybe.
Well, he looked at me with such a vicious look.
And he swung his arm.
He hit me so-- over my head-- so hard.
He says, get away.
And he put his hand, hand on his, on his gun.
He said, if you don't go, I kill you.
And I hear, I hear somebody in the back of me, move away, move away, a German, SS.
And I'm slightly moving.
He says, move away, move away, I tell you something.
He says, don't say nothing, just walk, walk.
I tell you something.
He says, keep walking.
Don't look at me.
He says, listen.
Go over to your sisters and tell them to move slowly back.
Because we just got a order that they don't have enough cattle cars for all the women.
So we're going to cut off some of them from the back.
Tell your sisters to move, but very slowly.
And don't look at me.
I walked over to my sisters.
And I told them.
And everybody started to call my name.
And it became very hectic, you know?
Everybody was calling and moving.
And all of the sudden, there's all the SS standing, starting to shoot in the air.
When Helen saw that I didn't come back, she went up stairs, woke up his mistress.
She says, listen, Susanna went down.
And she's not back.
We're going to lose her.
Because evidently, her sisters were there in the transport.
You have to get to the phone and call up the Jewish police to take the sisters out.
Otherwise, we were not going to have Susanna.
She says, I can't do this.
I can't do it without him.
I can't, I can't do it.
Please, don't make me do it.
Supposedly, Helen told me, she nagging her.
She says, you must do it.
We both working for you for long time.
You owe it to us.
You must do it.
She was in tears.
She says, please, don't let me do it.
But she forced her to do it.
And what happened, when my sisters were moving slightly back, simultaneously the Jewish policemens came and took them out through the back.
And that's how my sisters survive.
However, she told us in afternoon that she has to tell him one day because her conscience will bother her.
And we told her, please don't tell him.
It's over.
She says, no, I cannot live with it.
I must tell him.
But don't you worry.
I find a good time to tell him.
That a month later.
She was in bed.
And Helen said that this was-- she was almost leaving, because they went to bed.
And all of a sudden, he came looking for me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you imagine the fact that I wasn't there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because we were ordered to sleep not in the villa?
Would I have been there, I wouldn't be here telling [LAUGHS] you this story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That moment when I came up to the room when he rung that little bell, my feet were trembling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All he said to me, how lucky can a stupid dog be?
You are a stupid, lucky dog.
And I remembered what Helen told me, jawohl, Herr [?
Commandeer. ?] I saw her holding him very tight around his waist.
He said to make his coffee.
I went down.
I remember spilling the coffee because my hand were trembling.
I said, he's going to get dressed and go up to the camp and get my sisters.
And I waited a day, the next day, and the following day.
But he didn't go for them.
I think that, as I said from the beginning, it was in God's hands.
There was no reason.
There was no way to explain anything about him.
I only know that it was meant to be.
And I was fortunate to have my sisters.
I paid a big price, but thank God, I have my sisters.
Would I have more family?
Maybe I could do more.
Oh, yes.
I, I met a very-- I met a boy.
His name was Adam Sztap.
And I guess we were more just friends.
We really fell in love.
I met him when we were in the red house yet, in the red building downstairs, before the villa.
He was working in a small building that had the incoming and outcoming water and the waste from the camps.
And he was in charge of the pipes that were controlling the water.
And he worked at night.
And he was very involved in the resistance group, because there was a room behind those pipes that they were hiding arms.
And he was connected with one Ukrainian that he trusted.
And he used to bring him arms, ammunition.

And Adam [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually asked me a couple of times, and I refused, to help out the resistance group by bringing out some documents that were in Goeth's drawers and passes for people to be able to get in and out from the camp.
I never spoke about it until a book came out, was written by Dr.
Alexander Bibovich who had a photographic mind and remembered everything that happened in the ghetto, in the camp.
And he knew about our activity, about me, about Adam and others who were involved in that resistance group.
I didn't think I can speak about it, because it was so incredible for me to do what I did and taking such a big chances.
But after Adam asked me so many times, and I knew-- again, I was afraid that he will kill my sisters at the time, and other hundreds of hundreds of people-- that's what they used to do.
When one did something wrong, against the law, they would take 50, 100 people and shoot them.
That was his order.
But then when Adam was telling me that I am the only one to help, and in case they were going to kill us all, they will have enough arms to fight back.
And there were large pipes going out of the camp that some of us could, could run through and also deliver some of the arms.
And since that book came out, one of my friend told me that there is a book and there is a write-up about me, I do speak about it.
Because it's written in Polish.
Supposedly, they say it's also in Hebrew, in Israel.
I didn't see it, but I have the book.
And it says that the servant of Amon Goeth took big chances by taking out documents from Goeth's drawers in his room and also made copies of passes that helped the underground group.
Unfortunately, the [SIGHS] Ukrainian had denounced Adam Sztap to Goeth.
One day I was in the yard when Goeth came up on his white horse and stopped in front of the gate.
He said, Susanna, where is Adam?
I looked up at him.
I said, how does he know that I know Adam?
You see, there were some people that were denouncing things to him, unfortunately.
I looked at him with such surprise.
I said, I don't know.
He went off on his horse.
A couple of minutes later, I heard two shots.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My girlfriend told me-- she was there-- they brought Adam down.
He gave order to look for him.
He asked him, where do you keep your arms?
And Adam said, I don't have any arms.
He pulled out the gun.
[LAUGHS] Well, we'll continue on the next tape.
